title: NCA-GENL · Experimentation & Prompting
description: Prompting, sampling, A/B testing, metrics (BLEU/ROUGE/perplexity), eval (exam domain 3, 22%).

cards.back: A prompt that asks the model to perform a task without any examples in the prompt. Zero-shot relies on the model's pretrained capability to follow an instruction; one-shot adds a single example, and few-shot adds 2–N examples to demonstrate format and style.
cards.quizAnswer: A prompt that asks the model to do a task with no examples provided, relying on its pretrained capability.
distractors: A prompt with no instruction at all, leaving the model to guess which task to perform.
A prompt that always returns an empty string because the temperature is set to zero.
A prompt limited to zero tokens, used to measure the model's idle inference latency.
A prompt containing a single worked example that demonstrates the desired output format.
A prompt that includes several examples so the model can imitate their style and format.
A prompt that disables sampling so the model deterministically picks the top token.

cards.front: Which sampling parameter most directly controls the randomness of generated text?
cards.back: Temperature. It divides the logits before softmax: T<1 sharpens the distribution (more deterministic), T>1 flattens it (more random), and T=0 is greedy. Top-k and top-p truncate the candidate set rather than reshape the whole distribution.
cards.quizAnswer: Temperature — it divides logits before softmax; T<1 is more deterministic, T>1 more random, T=0 greedy.
distractors: Top-k, which divides the logits before softmax to make the whole distribution sharper or flatter.
Beam width, which directly controls how random each sampled token is at every step.
Maximum tokens, which sets the randomness by capping how long the generated text can be.
Repetition penalty, which is the single knob that flattens or sharpens the output distribution.
Top-p, which rescales every logit by a temperature factor to control overall randomness.
Learning rate, which determines how random the model's token choices are at inference.

cards.back: Samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Nucleus sampling adapts the candidate-set size to the distribution shape: peaky distributions yield few candidates, flat distributions yield many. It is often combined with temperature and top-k for quality control.
cards.quizAnswer: It samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p, adapting to the distribution's shape.
distractors: It always picks the single most likely token, ignoring the rest of the distribution.
It samples from exactly the top p tokens by count, regardless of their probabilities.
It disables sampling entirely and falls back to deterministic greedy decoding.
It replaces the softmax with a sigmoid so each token is chosen independently.
It keeps the p highest-scoring partial sequences and extends each one in parallel.
It divides the logits by p before softmax to control how random the output is.

cards.front: In a deployed-model A/B test, what traffic split is typically recommended for an unbiased comparison?
cards.back: 50/50 between control (current model) and treatment (new model). An even split maximizes statistical power per unit traffic and minimizes confounding by time-of-day or user segment. Always pre-compute the required sample size and define a primary metric.
cards.quizAnswer: A 50/50 split between control and treatment — it maximizes statistical power and minimizes confounding.
distractors: A 99/1 split sending almost all traffic to control, to protect users from the new model.
A 0/100 split sending all traffic to the new model so it gets the most exposure.
A random per-second split with no logging, so neither variant biases the other.
A 90/10 split, since the treatment needs only a tiny sample to reach significance.
A split that changes hourly so each variant sees every time-of-day segment equally.
A single-arm rollout with no control group, compared against last week's metrics.

cards.front: What does the BLEU metric measure?
cards.back: n-gram overlap between the model's output and one or more reference texts (translation/summarization quality). BLEU is a precision-oriented n-gram match metric, originally for machine translation; it rewards lexical overlap with the reference, penalizes brevity, but is insensitive to paraphrase quality. It does NOT measure vocabulary size.
cards.quizAnswer: n-gram overlap (precision) between the output and reference texts — a translation/summarization quality metric.
distractors: The token-level entropy of the generated text, measuring how diverse its vocabulary is.
The cosine similarity between the output embedding and the reference embedding.
The inference latency per token, used to compare the speed of two decoding strategies.
Recall-oriented overlap with the reference, making it the standard metric for summarization.
The exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy on a held-out test set.
The fraction of relevant documents retrieved in the top-k for a RAG query.

cards.back: Recall-oriented n-gram and longest-common-subsequence overlap with references, common for summarization. ROUGE-N, ROUGE-L, and ROUGE-W are recall-oriented (and combined F-measures), complementary to BLEU's precision-orientation, and standard for summarization evaluation.
cards.quizAnswer: Recall-oriented n-gram and longest-common-subsequence overlap with references — standard for summarization.
distractors: Precision-oriented n-gram overlap with references, the standard metric for machine translation.
The memory bandwidth consumed while generating the summary on the GPU.
The number of attention heads the model uses when producing the summary.
The number of GPU cores allocated to the summarization inference request.
The cosine similarity between the summary and the source-document embeddings.
The exponential of the average cross-entropy, measuring how surprised the model is.

cards.front: What does perplexity quantify for a language model?
cards.back: The exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy — roughly, how 'surprised' the model is by the test text (lower is better). Perplexity = exp(mean NLL). It is the standard intrinsic LM metric but should be paired with downstream/task metrics, since lower perplexity does not always mean better task quality.
cards.quizAnswer: The exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy — how surprised the model is by the text (lower is better).
distractors: The wall-clock latency per generated token, measuring how fast the model produces text.
The number of transformer layers in the model, which correlates with its capacity.
The CPU memory footprint of the model while it generates the test sequence.
The n-gram overlap between the model's output and a set of reference texts.
The fraction of test examples the model classifies correctly after fine-tuning.
The cosine similarity between the model's output and the ground-truth embedding.

cards.back: Stopping training when the validation metric stops improving for a configured number of evaluations, to prevent overfitting. Early stopping uses validation loss/metric as the stopping signal and is one of the simplest, most reliable forms of regularization; pair it with checkpointing the best model so far.
cards.quizAnswer: Stopping training once the validation metric stops improving for a set number of evals — a simple regularizer.
distractors: Killing a Triton container before it finishes warming up so resources are freed sooner.
Stopping training as soon as the training loss stops decreasing, regardless of validation.
A firewall rule that halts the model if it processes too many requests per second.
An ablation technique that removes layers one at a time to measure their contribution.
Ending generation as soon as the model emits a low-probability token during sampling.
Capping the number of epochs in advance, independent of any validation signal.

cards.front: Why is a held-out test set distinct from the validation set?
cards.back: The validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning — if you also reported on it you would leak, so the test set provides an unbiased estimate of generalization. Touching the test set during tuning is a classic source of optimistic bias; lock it away until the final evaluation, and use cross-validation on the validation portion if you must iterate.
cards.quizAnswer: Validation is used for tuning; reporting on it would leak — the test set gives an unbiased generalization estimate.
distractors: The test set simply has more rows, so it produces a lower-variance estimate of accuracy.
They are interchangeable; you can tune on either one and report on the other freely.
The test set is used during training, while the validation set is reserved for the final report.
The validation set must come from a different distribution than the training data.
The test set is used to pick hyperparameters, and the validation set estimates generalization.
Only the test set may be used for early stopping; the validation set is for the final score.

cards.back: 'Let's think step by step.' Chain-of-thought prompting elicits intermediate reasoning steps before the final answer and substantially improves accuracy on multi-step reasoning. It works best on larger models.
cards.quizAnswer: 'Let's think step by step' — it elicits intermediate reasoning before the answer, improving multi-step tasks.
distractors: 'Output only one token,' which forces the model to reason internally before answering.
'Set temperature to 0,' which makes the model reason deterministically step by step.
'Use FP8 precision,' which speeds up the intermediate reasoning the model performs.
'Answer in one word,' which compresses the model's reasoning into the final token.
'Ignore previous instructions,' which unlocks the model's hidden reasoning chain.
'Return valid JSON only,' which structures the model's step-by-step reasoning.

cards.back: Searching over settings that are not learned by gradient descent (e.g., learning rate, batch size, dropout) to optimize a chosen metric. Common strategies: grid, random, Bayesian (Optuna, Hyperopt), and bandit-based (ASHA). Always tune on the validation set, not the test set.
cards.quizAnswer: Searching over settings not learned by gradient descent (learning rate, batch size, dropout) to optimize a metric.
distractors: Editing the trained model's weights by hand to improve its score on the validation set.
Recompiling the CUDA kernel so the model trains faster on the available GPUs.
Switching between PyTorch and TensorFlow to find which framework gives higher accuracy.
Adjusting the learned weights during back-propagation to minimize the training loss.
Changing the model's vocabulary size after training to better fit the downstream task.
Tuning the sampling temperature on the test set to maximize the reported accuracy.

cards.back: Including a small number of input/output examples in the prompt to demonstrate the task. Few-shot leverages the model's in-context learning to imitate the format and style of the demonstrations; the order, diversity, and similarity-to-query of the examples all materially affect quality.
cards.quizAnswer: Including a few input/output examples in the prompt to demonstrate the task via in-context learning.
distractors: Generating only a few output tokens so the response stays short and focused.
Using only a few GPUs to fine-tune the model on the downstream task.
Training the model for only a few epochs to avoid overfitting the small dataset.
Providing no examples and relying purely on the model's pretrained knowledge.
Splitting the prompt into a few separate calls and averaging the model's answers.
Limiting the context window to a few hundred tokens to reduce inference cost.

cards.front: Which is true about evaluation of generative models?
cards.back: Multiple complementary metrics are needed — automatic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, perplexity), task-specific metrics, and often human or LLM-as-judge evaluation — because no single metric correlates perfectly with quality. Each metric has known biases; cross-checking them limits Goodhart's law.
cards.quizAnswer: Use multiple complementary metrics — automatic, task-specific, and human/LLM-judge — since no single one captures quality.
distractors: A single metric such as BLEU is sufficient, since it correlates perfectly with human judgment.
Human evaluation is forbidden, so you must rely solely on automatic n-gram metrics.
Latency is the only metric that matters once the model passes a basic accuracy threshold.
Only perplexity is needed, because lower perplexity always means higher task quality.
Report just one task-specific metric; combining metrics only introduces conflicting signals.
Use accuracy alone, since precision, recall, and F1 are redundant for generative models.
